Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd set aside time to study, maybe around 15 hours a week, but I often found myself just going through the motions rather than really digging in. I preferred reading and writing things out, so I'd print materials or copy notes into documents. Some weeks I was more focused, other weeks the sadness just made everything feel like wading through mud.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on how I was feeling emotionally. When anxiety was really hitting me hard, I couldn't stick to any schedule even if I wanted to. Deadlines would force me into action, but regular consistent timing? That wasn't really my thing this semester. I'd have bursts of productivity followed by days where I could barely bring myself to open my laptop.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something I'd missed, which fed into my anxiety about falling behind. I wasn't someone who logged in out of curiosity or genuine interest most of the time—it was more obligation and fear of missing something important.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a comfortable habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, but it always felt like a chore rather than something natural. The closest it got to routine was Sunday evenings when I'd force myself to see what was due for the week ahead.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs, highlight them, and make my own notes in Word documents. The discussion boards were okay when other students actually posted thoughtful responses—those peer interactions helped me see different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, even though there were a lot of them available. Something about sitting through video content just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out and then have to rewatch sections. I'd rather read a transcript if one was available. I also ignored a lot of the optional supplementary materials because I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. When I could tell that doing something would actually help my grade, I'd push through even when I didn't feel like it. Vague enrichment activities? Those got pushed aside because I just didn't have the mental energy for anything extra.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford emotionally. Required work already took so much out of me that adding anything optional seemed impossible. There was always this guilt about not doing them, which just added to my anxiety, but I had to prioritize or I'd completely fall apart.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed readings, handwritten notes, and my own typed summaries. I'd download everything I could and then work from those files without being connected to the LMS. I also used some external websites and my textbooks quite a bit.
Q10: There were many times I spent hours reviewing my printed notes and rewriting key concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd sit at my desk working through practice problems on paper, or I'd read chapters multiple times to really understand them. The LMS only saw me download something once, not the five times I reviewed it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or document once, and then that became my primary study material for days or weeks. I preferred having everything saved locally so I didn't have to deal with internet issues or the stress of navigating the platform. My laptop was basically my offline study hub.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and everything became harder. When I was feeling particularly sad, even simple tasks felt overwhelming. Around midterms, I hit a wall where I was attending less, completing fewer assignments, and just feeling disconnected from everything. Those were dark weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I had too much going on or was mentally exhausted, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but the fatigue would pile up. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes pulled my attention away, and confusion about course expectations made me avoid logging in altogether.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my mind was somewhere else, usually spiraling about other worries. I'd technically complete a reading but couldn't tell you what it said five minutes later. That happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—like there was this invisible barrier. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, but that headspace rarely came. By the time I'd start, I was already stressed about the deadline.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly log in multiple times, frantically trying to catch up on everything I'd been avoiding. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was sometimes the only thing that could cut through the fog.
Q17: When I got good feedback on my assignments—and my assignment scores were actually pretty high—it gave me a temporary boost. It reminded me I was capable even when I didn't feel like it. But the effect wore off quickly, and I'd slip back into my usual patterns of avoidance and anxiety.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace sometimes, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. Having nine online courses meant a lot of self-direction, and I wasn't always capable of providing that for myself.
Q19: Clear deadlines and straightforward instructions made engagement easier. What made it harder was when expectations were vague or when there were too many different platforms and resources to keep track of. My anxiety would spike when I wasn't sure exactly what was expected of me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in frequently out of anxiety, checking and rechecking things, but then struggles to actually complete and submit work. The mental barrier between engaging with materials and producing something to turn in can be huge. Activity doesn't equal productivity when you're struggling.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores and assignment quality were decent when I actually submitted things, even though my LMS activity was pretty low. I did a lot of learning offline, through my own notes and reading. The platform didn't capture the real studying I was doing away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or avoidance. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the clicks, and all the offline work. They can't see that someone downloaded a file once but studied it for ten hours over the next week.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I feel connected to what I'm learning and can see its relevance. This semester, I had moments of real engagement, but they were often overshadowed by anxiety and sadness that made everything feel distant.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still trying to keep up. It can't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my anxiety, or the tears before logging in, or the relief when I finally submitted something. It also can't show that my high assignment scores came from genuine effort and understanding, even if my overall engagement looked low.
